Notes App Excerpt: Hormel Pepperoni Guy One Man Band Depression
Ode to Mennel Ibtissam singing Hallelujah on The Voice (France), translated in Arabic
Notes for a Film on Black Joy
In a dream I watch your small body

MATERNAL INSTINCTS Restless howl buffets my throat. Nobody told me that to be a mother is to swallow a gale, let it build a tempest behind your teeth. On the other side: small flame of my child, guttering.

DARK When you have a baby there is a certain time in the morning- maybe 3am-that conjures Dark no matter the kitchen's microwave- neon glow. Kind of Dark that whispers, You are the only person who has ever wanted to smother your children. I know this because I have been that mother, holding a hand above my crying child. Clock snagged on that endless hour. Dark balancing on the back of my palm

SONNET FOR ISIS Before she bore her only son, Isis raised up his father from the dead. She sought his pieces round the world: arm drifted down the Nile, three ribs caught in the corpse of a struck tree. Built him up from sand, & sorrow, & sorcery. When Horus wails, upon joining her with the living, does Isis doubt her choice? When her milk is slow or when her broken body spills itself into the sheets; when she forgets again to feed herself; when there is no one else to lift her son-god from her arms. When she flees from sickness & scorpions to keep him safe. When she forgives him her own beheading.

IN A DREAM I WATCH YOUR SMALL BODY FOLD UNDER THE WHEELS OF A CAB Did you know a piece of paper can only be folded seven times? Eight if you hire a steamroller. Eleven if you are an award- winning science fair student with half a mile of toilet paper. If you could, forty-two folds would take you to the moon; one hundred & three to the edge of the universe. Try it. Seven folds: all you'll ever get
// Tomorrow's purple morning calls us from our grief; old year, still reaching

THE MONTH WE GET MARRIED three whales beach themselves to rot. Up north, a school is rechristened a graveyard. There are eighteen-year-old boys cosplaying bravery, fighting for the chance to die in someone else's hometown. A father floats his son across a river that was supposed to save them both. Somewhere a daughter goes missing for the last time. This isn't even the worst of it. Every day there's a new reason to think I don't believe this, but of course, I do.

I once read a book about a woman attacked by a bear. She was attacked by a bear & lived-do you believe it? She lived & years later took her own life, instead. My Opa lived, too-dropped an anvil on his foot to escape the Eastern Front; moved to Canada to escape the wrong side of the wall. Moved to Canada & bought a marina, of all things. He left that marina only once: flew to Thailand to buy a sailboat, even though he couldn't sail. You can't sail, my Oma reminded him. He came home (without the sailboat) & drowned: one day he went down to the empty dock & kept going straight off the end. I do believe he survived as long as he could

HOSPICE When she doesn't come for Christmas dinner my parents do that thing where they won't say what's going on. The year turns itself over. Now she's in hospice & won't let anyone visit- doesn't want us kids to see her like this- but my teachers don't know that. I skip class, get caught; I'm forgiven. The rest of the month the school lets my grandmother die undistracted. Afterwards, the nurses tell us they helped her put her cowboy boots on every morning. Yes, even on the days she couldn't get out of bed. The night she passed, they say, we heard her laughing in her sleep. At first it feels like a terrible goodbye. Some days, I'm still forgiving her for it.
PINE BEETLE KILL "Artisans promoting silver lining in blue pine" -The Globe & Mail, 17 January

Summer pants in too early, smoke-swollen, dragging your parched childhood. Like the August you all slept in the basement's black chill, or the mornings you licked ash off the spoons left out overnight. You are an adult now, with your own kids; how are you not worried the garden centre is selling citrus & pomegranate trees instead of frost peach & hardy pear? That pine beetles, mild- winter coddled, are eating the forests blue? Someone calls it denim pine as if it is something to covet. You've been fooled: potted lemon, navy bench, your children stirring ash into every bitter glass

VICTIMS IDENTIFIED Next morning we read that the crash was the equivalent of driving more than a hundred miles an hour. We learn that the paramedics got lost on the highway's lonely dark, though everyone agrees to believe it wouldn't have mattered. Maybe there is nothing more to tell. Soon only the families cast their names like coins, knowing wishing won't make it so (but-what if it could?) The world creaks & turns gathering speed.

PURPLE There is a war happening & it feels so distant even though I am watching it from my palm. I don't look like one side, or share words with the other-just one more white mother wringing her hands at each new post that wants me to believe that this child didn't deserve to die & that is why this other child is also dead. A bag of crocus bulbs is musting on my porch because I cannot put my phone down long enough to plant them. Come spring, the garden will grow regardless. Even though all those purple faces are missing, even though all of them (all of them) should have been here

POEM Lately everything's a poem I just can't write: the dust, the sandals. Rugs masquerading as shrouds. Every one of those shrouds is a poem. See, right there- I wrote poem, but what I meant was person.

HOUSEGUEST after Callista Buchen Invited for the weekend, my despair is still here five months later. She loads the dishwasher without scraping the plates. When it rains, she lines up the children's boots by the door where (of course) they can't find them; on sunny days, she critiques the garden from the window. Any time I try to work she makes herself a snack & sits at the table with me, reading the news, making those soft sounds that one makes when someone far away has died. I know she's trying: adds cereal to the grocery list but forgets milk, runs the dryer too short & leaves the clothes to sour. I come to bed late, bearing damp matched socks. She rolls without waking, offers her own warm ache
// There comes a scuttling grey. We built a lighthouse for this dark, any storm

FRIENDLY BEASTS On Christmas Eve, animals speak. This is the story I tell to lure you into the world. Wiping scales from your back, I ignore your fleeting tail. I know not to search for threat of venom or fang in the gift of your mouth. You land on my breast, bare of feathers or fur, ruffle your wings & find them clipped, press your harmless paws into a human mother. I wonder when you realize my trick. I had to, you see. Who would agree to be born a child? You howl & howl. Nobody answers but me.

BIRTH DAY 1// Close your eyes, keep them dark for hours. All the helping hands in the room are not, in fact, helpful at all-only watching. You do not see this. You are listening for a knock. When it comes, your only job is to push open the door. Here's the trick: you have to open it Only you. with your whole body. Only yours. 2// Keep your eyes open: this is the one thing you can do. You cannot help open the door; it is not yours to pass through. Whatever you see, you can only wait. Watch the door shudder & shake. Schroedinger's door: on the other side there is nothing, yet. On the other side there is everything. 3// Here there is nothing to see. Instead, your eyes reach for bright heat at the end of a long hall & you know your family is there, just beyond the door. Later the three of you will each have different memories of how long you waited to cross the threshold into the world of the dying. Much later, at the end of your death, you will rejoin that same light; be forgiven for still calling it life

ODE TO MY MARRIAGE AFTER KIDS We're having the same fight: you know the one, the one where I cry, feel misunderstood; the one where you feel like nothing you do is ever enough. The kind of fight that should be shouted, doors slammed. Instead we trade grated whispers, frowning on the farthest edges of the couch because down the hall, doors still open, our children are supposed to be asleep. See, when you have children every fight is a little bit about them. I think, I don't want this fight again & again. I imagine their small rooms, half-empty-I imagine there were never any small rooms at all- I take it back, I'll take it all, this fight my whole life, if that is the price, of course I'll pay it. Of course I'll stay.

SEX AFTER BABY I do remember the wild gallop of my youth, cantering into any kind hand. Unbroken filly. Easy roan. She's suffered the same absent rider for years. Stabled between my ribs, grown slow & mostly forgotten. Sometimes I let her out anyways: one run around an empty paddock. Watch her eyes slide as she passes the gate. I need her to know that well-loved horses can live into their sixties. I need her to believe she'll outlast this rider yet

INSTRUCTIONS FOR FALLING IN LOVE after Joy Sullivan "What Farrant saw was a 'resurrection plant,' which can survive with no water for months to years. Seeing how quickly the plants recover is a remarkable sight." -BBC, 20 December

When you are not sure yet: spend time somewhere public (a library will do, a yoga class). Gird your limbs. Weigh how much they ache. Let him feed you what you've never eaten. Measure your legs against his: standing, sitting, side-by-side. (This should be the fun part.) Buy a couch together, a car. Drive everything you cherish to a new city. If you are going to share a lifetime of good fights, start practicing now. Write your promises to each other on scrap paper. Frame them; move them carefully from home to home. Watch them fade in the light of each new day. Fall out of love, repeatedly: when money or time are tight, when the kids are small & not sleeping. Remember that love can be a rose of Jericho: alive for years on the memory of water.
// Now the kettle sings its boiling song. I pour two mugs full, forgetting

IN PRAISE OF SWEETBREAD My Oma cooked everything: tongue & tripe, snapped bone, split shell. I think of my mother, meeting this woman's cooking for the first time. Setting her honeyed toast at the door, thinking it would taste the same when she left. The flames that became my sister & I took a long time to light. My Oma died believing no amount of liver nor roe could coax us to life. Water unboiled & cold in the commercial-kitchen-sized stockpot that my parents inherited, big enough to bathe these babies that will never eat sweetbread, missing, as we are, someone who knows how to cook it for us.

SIDE EFFECTS MAY INCLUDE Two seals float the wavewashed highway, penny-bright. My high school boyfriend hands me his infant son & dives. Always wanted to swim with dolphins; this must be close enough. His child cries in Japanese & still he refuses to abandon the slick of his old wish. In the dusk a small bell sounds, hanging from the improbable corner of a door ajar. The bell tastes like a rusted ache, bitten quick. It reminds me that we do not understand each other, me & this baby that cannot be mine. That my moon has pulled the tide to its wintermidnight low for years. Even so: my feet feel wet. Only one seal returns, waiting. I know I should give back the baby, but I cannot move my arms

AFTER MY HUSBAND'S VASECTOMY I stop taking the pill for the third time in a decade. Belly a wound unsure of how to bleed. This time, there are no pregnancy tests expectant under the sink; the morning circles the drain while we wait for the pharmacy to open, looking up failure rates. My husband is sure all our kids are already in the living room. We each hold our own breath. He thinks we are making the same wish.

PHOSPHENE In my dream my friend is still pregnant with her third child. My jealousy: a crimson moon, its ravenous tide. I wanted another baby, too. I want another baby. Another baby would mean another marriage, but I like this marriage most days. I like my garden, twin coffees in the morning. I like sharing the bed delivered the day I went into labour with my son. The baby I don't have is a phosphene only I can see, although in the dream I wonder if my friend knows & that is why she keeps the table between me & her belly. Sometimes I feel early kicks bubbling my palms; like the phosphenes, they are real only to me. In my dream I am the one who has been cheated. I wake up not knowing what to do with the wound behind my eyes, the blood that isn't mine

LEAVING THE HOSPITAL WITHOUT A BABY The summer before, there is a woman at the market turning every leaf into tea: blackberry, bee balm, red raspberry-for later. Did you know apple leaf tea smells just like apple cider? Unmistakably an apple tree even without her fruit.

PIN Even now my PIN's the last four digits of my childhood best friend's home phone number. There's a lot from my childhood that doesn't exist anymore. But maybe someone is still carrying me, too: a little girl with thin brown hair in their wallet. On the lock screen of their phone, a bike lock combination. Zeno's paradox: we grow up halfway, then halfway again. Mathematically, we never finish growing. Mathematically, we never

CHIMES In a parallel universe, I hang your wind chimes on the other porch, away from the worrying southwind that wakes us too early. I leave first every morning in the old blue Volvo I will not sell, not even with its empty air conditioning rattle. In this universe you do not let me explain. When you leave, I rake my grief across a few strange men, eventually meet a different husband. We get married on the lake where he grew up, not my childhood ocean. Later we have two kids, too- both girls this time. The wind blows from the north; your chimes are mostly quiet.

BRAIN TREE When my son is young & trying not to go to bed, he tells me there is a tree in his brain & on it are all his thoughts growing as different-coloured fruits: angry red cherries, sad blueberries. Embarrassing thoughts are soft yellow pears. Happy: tangerines. He asks me to tell him the most sour-cherry thought I ever had. Afterwards he insists it was unripe, barely pink. Which is fair-I didn't tell him about the time I imagined the shape my knuckles would leave on a cheek. I didn't tell him I imagined his cheek. My branches are heavy with limes, dragonfruit, pale lychee; gooseberry & plums. Bitter melon. Wasp-stung figs. I lie beside him while he doesn't sleep. In the dark I reach for oranges, kumquats, apricots, persimmon. We eat them whole, swallow the seeds
// Fingers scatterrush over the evening's many feasts. There is so much to taste & I have only these two hands
